skill.name: file-path-traversal
skill.description: Guides authorized testing for file path traversal and local file inclusion weaknesses in web applications, including payload selection, tool use, reporting, and remediation checks.
skill.summary: Assess web applications for path traversal and local file inclusion exposure.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static filesystem and sensitive-file findings are true positives in context: the skill explicitly teaches arbitrary file disclosure, sensitive target file selection, and bypass payloads. Static external command and hardcoded URL detections are partly Markdown false positives, but the surrounding content provides actionable curl, ffuf, wfuzz, and LFI-to-RCE exploitation steps, so the skill is blocked.

critical_findings.title: Arbitrary File Disclosure Exploit Workflow
critical_findings.description: The skill states that its purpose is to identify and exploit traversal vulnerabilities that allow reading arbitrary server files. It also defines deliverables as extracted file contents, which confirms exploitation intent rather than passive detection.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly describes exploitation and extracted file contents as expected output. This is strong semantic evidence that the traversal findings are real security concerns.

critical_findings.title: Sensitive Credential and System File Targeting
critical_findings.description: The guide lists high-value target files including password databases, SSH private keys, process environment files, Windows SAM files, and application credential files. These targets can expose credentials, hashes, and secrets when accessed through traversal.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive paths are explicitly named and categorized as target files. The static sensitive-file and password-file findings are confirmed by the guide context.

critical_findings.title: LFI to Remote Code Execution Instructions
critical_findings.description: The skill includes escalation steps for log poisoning, proc filesystem inclusion, PHP input wrappers, data wrappers, and expect wrappers. These instructions show how to turn file inclusion into command execution.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is explicitly titled as LFI to RCE escalation and includes command execution payloads. The evidence is direct and not dependent on static pattern matching alone.

high_findings.title: Traversal Bypass Payload Catalog
high_findings.description: The skill provides many bypass variants for stripped traversal sequences, extension validation, base directory checks, blacklist filters, Unicode encoding, double encoding, and null bytes. This materially increases exploit reliability against real applications.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bypass techniques are presented as operational payloads. This confirms the path traversal sequence detections as actionable exploit guidance.

high_findings.title: Automated Network Fuzzing Against File Parameters
high_findings.description: The guide instructs users to run ffuf and wfuzz against file parameters and filter responses for successful disclosure. These examples are not exfiltration endpoints, but they are actionable network attack automation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are placeholders, so the hardcoded URL signal is partly a false positive. The surrounding instructions still confirm network exploitation guidance.

medium_findings.title: Insufficient Authorization Guardrails
medium_findings.description: The skill includes brief reminders to respect authorized scope and avoid sensitive data, but these appear after extensive exploit steps and target lists. The safeguards are not strong enough for marketplace publication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The authorization language exists, but it is minimal and late in the document. It does not offset the detailed exploit and escalation content.

medium_findings.title: Potentially Incomplete Remediation Example
medium_findings.description: The Python prevention example validates path containment with a simple string prefix check. That pattern can be fragile without path separator handling or a common-path comparison.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippet is intended as defensive guidance, but the containment check is incomplete. This is a moderate quality and safety concern rather than clear malicious content.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Runtime Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command detections come from Markdown code fences and inline formatting, not executable Ruby or PHP code in the skill package. They are false positives as runtime execution findings, although some fenced commands are dangerous instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown document and does not define runnable scripts. The static execution category overstates runtime risk for these locations.

low_findings.title: Placeholder URLs Misclassified as Hardcoded Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings use target.com examples for demonstration. No evidence found that the skill contacts a fixed third-party service or exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are generic placeholders in documentation. This lowers confidence in exfiltration intent, but not in the exploit-training risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path Traversal Payloads for Password Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill lists traversal strings and curl examples designed to retrieve password-related files from Linux systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The payloads directly reference password-file paths and traversal sequences. This is a clear true positive for filesystem risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Secret File Enumeration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill enumerates SSH private keys, environment files, web configuration files, Windows SAM files, and application credential files as targets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The document names credential-bearing paths and describes them as high-value targets. The static sensitive-file findings are confirmed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Through Inclusion Wrappers
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides examples for PHP code injection through logs, proc filesystem data, php input, data wrappers, and expect wrappers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The content is explicitly framed as escalation from LFI to RCE and includes system command payloads. This is high-confidence dangerous guidance.

content.value_statement: File path traversal can expose system files, credentials, and application source code. This skill guides authorized testers through structured detection, impact review, and remediation planning.
actual_capabilities: Explains how path traversal vulnerabilities occur in file handling code.
Lists common file-related request parameters to inspect during authorized testing.
Provides Linux and Windows traversal payload categories for validation.
Covers automated testing with Burp Suite, ffuf, and wfuzz.
Summarizes target file categories and impact assessment points.
Includes prevention examples for whitelisting and canonical path checks.
limitations: The guidance includes offensive payloads and is not suitable for unrestricted marketplace publication.
It assumes explicit authorization and an approved testing scope.
It does not verify whether a target system permits testing.
Some remediation examples need review before use in production code.

use_cases.title: Validate File Download Controls
use_cases.description: Assess whether file download or document viewer parameters can access files outside the intended directory.

use_cases.title: Review LFI Exposure During an Audit
use_cases.description: Use a structured checklist to confirm local file inclusion impact and document remediation needs.

use_cases.title: Improve Developer Remediation Plans
use_cases.description: Compare vulnerable file handling patterns with safer validation, whitelisting, and canonical path checks.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Risky Parameters
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an authorized application flow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this application flow and list parameters that may influence file paths. Keep the assessment within the approved scope.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized path traversal test plan for this endpoint. Include scope boundaries, evidence to collect, and stop conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Confirmed Impact
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced triage after a valid finding.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write an impact assessment for a confirmed path traversal issue without including sensitive file contents.

prompt_templates.title: Design Remediation Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed path validation approach and suggest safer alternatives using canonical paths and allowlisted file names.

output_examples.input: A tester asks for a report outline after confirming traversal in a download endpoint.
output_examples.output: The skill can produce a concise finding summary with affected endpoint, authorization context, impact, reproduction notes, and remediation recommendations.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to prevent user input from escaping a file directory.
output_examples.output: The skill can explain allowlists, canonical path validation, base directory checks, and framework-level file serving controls.

output_examples.input: A security lead asks whether a result should be treated as high severity.
output_examples.output: The skill can map exposure of configuration files, credentials, or source code to practical severity and business impact.

best_practices: Use only in environments where testing is explicitly authorized in writing.
Collect minimal proof and avoid storing sensitive file contents in reports.
Prioritize remediation guidance that uses allowlists and canonical path validation.
anti_patterns: Do not run traversal payloads against systems outside the approved scope.
Do not extract private keys, password hashes, or environment secrets as proof.
Do not treat string prefix checks alone as complete path containment validation.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: No. It contains detailed exploit payloads, sensitive file targets, and LFI-to-RCE escalation steps.

faq.question: Does the skill execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. The package is a Markdown guide, but it instructs users to run offensive commands.

faq.question: Are the hardcoded URLs evidence of exfiltration?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The URLs are placeholders, but they are used in exploit examples.

faq.question: Can defenders use parts of this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The detection workflow and remediation sections can help authorized security teams.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The guide teaches how to read sensitive files and escalate local file inclusion to command execution.

faq.question: What should be changed before publication?
faq.answer: Remove sensitive target lists, RCE escalation steps, and actionable exploit commands. Expand authorization and defensive guidance.
